walkthrough.title: Choose France or Ireland
walkthrough.text: Before we set anything up, let's pick a variant. Ora et Labora comes with two different building sets: France and Ireland. France focuses on wine production and ornate cloisters, while Ireland features whiskey distilleries and a slightly different building mix. The core rules are identical, so pick whichever theme appeals to your group.

walkthrough.title: Assemble the Production Wheel
walkthrough.text: Let's find the production wheel. This is the large central board with a rotating arm. Place it in the middle of the table where everyone can reach it. This is the heart of the game and tracks how plentiful each resource is at any given moment.

walkthrough.title: Set the Wheel Starting Positions
walkthrough.text: Place the goods markers on the production wheel at their starting positions. Each good has its own track on the wheel. As the game progresses and the wheel advances, the production value of each good increases by one per round. When someone takes a good, its marker resets to zero.

walkthrough.title: Sort the Building Cards
walkthrough.text: Now let's sort the building cards. Separate them by the letter on their back, which indicates when they enter the game. Place the starting buildings face up in a display where everyone can see them. Stack the remaining buildings by their letter designation to be introduced in later rounds.

walkthrough.title: Prepare the Landscape Tiles
walkthrough.text: Find all the landscape tiles and sort them by type: forest, moor, coastal, hillside, and plains. Stack each type in its own pile near the central area. Players will purchase these throughout the game to expand their territories.

walkthrough.title: Set Up the Resource Supply
walkthrough.text: Gather all the resource tokens and organize them into a general supply. You will need wood, clay, peat, grain, livestock, coins, and all the processed goods like flour, bread, wine, beer, whiskey, meat, ceramic, bricks, straw, ornaments, books, and relics. Keep them sorted so players can easily find what they need.

walkthrough.title: Prepare the Settlement Tiles
walkthrough.text: Find the settlement tiles and set them in a supply near the building display. These tiles are placed during settlement scoring phases to mark which groups of buildings qualify as settlements. Each settlement has specific requirements that must be met.

walkthrough.title: Place the Round Marker
walkthrough.text: Put the round marker on the first space of the round track. The game lasts either twenty-four or twenty-five rounds depending on your player count and variant. The round track also shows when new buildings become available and when settlement scoring occurs.

walkthrough.title: Distribute Heartland Strips
walkthrough.text: Each player takes one heartland strip. This is your starting landscape, a small grid of terrain including a couple of forest and moor tiles. Place it in front of you. This is the foundation of your monastery estate, and you will expand it throughout the game.

walkthrough.title: Place Starting Buildings
walkthrough.text: Each player places a small set of starting buildings onto their heartland. These are basic structures like a Farmyard and a Clay Mound that give you your first production options. Place them on the designated spaces of your heartland strip as shown in the rules.

walkthrough.title: Distribute Clergy Members
walkthrough.text: Each player receives three clergy members: one Prior and two Lay Brothers. These are your workers for the entire game. Place them near your heartland where you can easily reach them. You will never gain additional clergy, so managing these three workers is a core part of the game.

walkthrough.title: Hand Out Starting Resources
walkthrough.text: Give each player their starting resources. The exact allocation varies slightly by turn order. The first player receives fewer coins, while later players receive compensation coins to balance going second, third, or fourth. Check the setup chart in the rules for the exact amounts.

walkthrough.title: Determine First Player
walkthrough.text: Choose a starting player by any method you prefer. Play will proceed clockwise. Remember that later players receive extra starting coins to compensate for going later in the first round.

walkthrough.title: Review the Building Display
walkthrough.text: Take a moment as a group to look at the buildings currently available. Each building shows its cost, its effect when used, and its victory point value. Familiarize yourself with what you can build early on. New buildings will become available as the game progresses through the round track.

walkthrough.title: Final Setup Check
walkthrough.text: Let's do a quick check. Everyone should have a heartland strip with starting buildings, three clergy members, and some starting resources. The production wheel should be assembled in the center with goods markers at their starting positions. The building display, landscape tiles, settlement tiles, and resource supply should all be accessible. Ready to play? Let's learn the rules.

walkthrough.title: Game Overview
walkthrough.text: Ora et Labora, which means "Pray and Work," is a resource management game about building a medieval monastery. Over twenty-four to twenty-five rounds, you will harvest resources, construct buildings, and form settlements. The unique production wheel means goods become more plentiful over time, creating tension about when to harvest.

walkthrough.title: Round Structure
walkthrough.text: Each round has a simple structure. First, advance the production wheel one notch, which increases every good's production value by one. Then the active player takes exactly one action. Play passes clockwise until every player has taken one action, and then a new round begins.

walkthrough.title: The Four Actions
walkthrough.text: On your turn, you choose exactly one of four actions. You can fell trees or cut peat from your landscape. You can use a building by placing a clergy member on it. You can build a new building and add it to your territory. Or you can buy a landscape strip to expand your territory. Let's walk through each one.

walkthrough.title: Action: Fell Trees or Cut Peat
walkthrough.text: This is your basic resource gathering. Remove a forest tile from your landscape to gain wood, or remove a moor tile to gain peat. The amount you gain equals the current value on the production wheel for that good, and then the wheel resets that good to zero. This means waiting longer gives you more, but someone else might take it first.

walkthrough.title: The Production Wheel in Detail
walkthrough.text: Let's pause and really understand the wheel, because it drives the whole game. Every round, all goods increase by one. When anyone takes a production action for a good, they receive the accumulated amount and it resets. If wood is at five and you fell trees, you get five wood. Then wood resets to zero and starts climbing again next round.

walkthrough.title: Timing the Wheel
walkthrough.text: The wheel creates a core tension. You want to wait for values to build up, but if you wait too long, another player might take the good first and reset it. Watching what your opponents need and timing your harvests is one of the most important skills in the game.

walkthrough.title: Action: Use a Building
walkthrough.text: To use a building, place one of your three clergy members on it and activate its effect. Buildings let you convert resources, gain goods, trade, and more. You can use your own buildings for free. You can also use an opponent's building, but you must pay them a fee.

walkthrough.title: Clergy Placement Rules
walkthrough.text: You have three clergy: one Prior and two Lay Brothers. Your Prior can be placed on any building, including opponents' buildings. Lay Brothers can only be placed on your own buildings. A building can only hold one clergy member at a time, so if a building already has someone on it, nobody else can use it until that clergy is removed.

walkthrough.title: Clergy Recall
walkthrough.text: Here is a crucial rule. Once all three of your clergy are placed on buildings, you must recall all of them before placing any again. This means you cannot use buildings until you get your workers back. Timing your placements so you do not run out of clergy at a critical moment is essential.

walkthrough.title: Using Opponents' Buildings
walkthrough.text: You can use an opponent's building by placing your Prior on it and paying them a usage fee. This gives you access to buildings you have not built yourself. However, it costs you money and ties up your Prior. It also blocks that building for the opponent until your clergy is recalled.

walkthrough.title: Action: Build a Building
walkthrough.text: To build, choose an available building from the display and pay its resource cost. Then place the building tile on an empty valid space in your landscape grid. Different buildings require different terrain types. Once built, the building is yours for the rest of the game and can be used by placing clergy on it.

walkthrough.title: Building Types
walkthrough.text: Buildings come in several categories. Production buildings let you harvest or generate basic goods. Processing buildings convert raw goods into more valuable ones. Civic buildings provide various economic benefits. Religious buildings, or cloisters, offer powerful abilities and score bonus points based on adjacency.

walkthrough.title: Processing Chains
walkthrough.text: Processing is how you turn cheap raw goods into valuable ones. Grain goes to flour at the mill, then flour becomes bread at the bakery using energy. Livestock becomes meat at the slaughterhouse. Clay becomes ceramic or bricks at the pottery. In France, grapes become wine. In Ireland, grain becomes whiskey. These processed goods are worth significantly more points.

walkthrough.title: Energy for Processing
walkthrough.text: Many processing buildings require energy to operate. Wood, peat, and coal all serve as energy sources. When a building requires energy, you spend the appropriate fuel tokens along with the raw material. Planning your energy supply alongside your raw materials is important.

walkthrough.title: Action: Buy a Landscape
walkthrough.text: You can spend coins to purchase an additional landscape strip and attach it to your territory. Coastal, hillside, and plains strips each provide different terrain for building placement. More territory means more room for buildings and larger settlements.

walkthrough.title: Territory Layout
walkthrough.text: Your territory is arranged in a grid pattern. When you buy a new landscape strip, you attach it to your existing grid, expanding it in one direction. Buildings must be placed on valid terrain within this grid. Planning your layout so buildings end up adjacent to each other for settlement formation is critical.

walkthrough.title: Settlement Scoring Overview
walkthrough.text: At fixed points during the game, settlement scoring occurs. A settlement is a connected group of buildings that meets specific requirements, including at least one dwelling or house. You place a settlement tile on the group to mark it. Each settlement scores points based on the value of the buildings it contains.

walkthrough.title: Settlement Requirements
walkthrough.text: Each settlement must include specific types of buildings to qualify. The requirements vary, but generally you need a dwelling and a mix of economic and religious buildings. Buildings in a settlement must be connected, meaning they share edges in your landscape grid. You can form multiple settlements in your territory.

walkthrough.title: Settlement Scoring Timing
walkthrough.text: The round track shows exactly when settlement scoring happens. When a scoring round arrives, each player may form settlements and score them. You should be planning for these scoring rounds several turns in advance. Buildings not part of any settlement may score reduced or zero points.

walkthrough.title: Cloisters and Adjacency
walkthrough.text: Cloisters are special religious buildings that score bonus points based on what is adjacent to them in your grid. Placing a cloister surrounded by high-value buildings maximizes its bonus. France and Ireland have different cloister sets, but both reward careful spatial planning.

walkthrough.title: New Buildings Entering Play
walkthrough.text: As the game progresses, new and more powerful buildings become available at specific rounds marked on the round track. Later buildings are more expensive but also more powerful and worth more victory points. Keeping an eye on what is coming helps you plan your resource accumulation.

walkthrough.title: Coins and Economy
walkthrough.text: Coins are used to buy landscapes, pay building usage fees to opponents, and sometimes as part of building costs. Some buildings generate coins when used. Coins do not count directly as victory points, so you want to spend them strategically rather than hoarding them.

walkthrough.title: Game End
walkthrough.text: The game ends after the final round, which is round twenty-four or twenty-five. One last settlement scoring phase occurs. Then you tally up all your points from settlement scoring throughout the game, building values, and goods conversion.

walkthrough.title: Final Scoring
walkthrough.text: Your final score is the sum of three categories. First, all points earned from settlement scoring phases throughout the game. Second, the printed victory point value of every building in your territory. Third, points from converting high-value goods like wine, whiskey, relics, books, and ornaments at their specified rates. The player with the most points wins, with remaining goods as tiebreaker.

walkthrough.title: Welcome to the Monastery
walkthrough.text: Welcome to your first game of Ora et Labora! In this practice tutorial, we will walk through a scripted opening with three example players. Each player will take different approaches so you can see how the game's key mechanisms work in practice. Follow along even if your player count differs. Let's begin.

walkthrough.title: Starting Position
walkthrough.text: Each player has their heartland strip with a couple of forest and moor tiles, three clergy members, and some starting resources. The production wheel is at its starting position, with all goods at low values. Take a moment to look at the buildings available in the initial display.

walkthrough.title: Round One: Wheel Advances
walkthrough.text: The first round begins. Advance the production wheel one notch. Every good on the wheel ticks up by one. This is the rhythm of the entire game. Each round, goods become slightly more valuable, creating mounting pressure about when to harvest.

walkthrough.title: Player One: Fell Trees
walkthrough.text: Player one starts by felling trees. They remove a forest tile from their heartland and gain wood equal to the current wheel value. It is only round one, so the value is low, but Player one needs wood right now to build an early building. Sometimes you cannot afford to wait for the wheel to accumulate.

walkthrough.title: Player Two: Cut Peat
walkthrough.text: Player two takes a similar approach but cuts peat instead. They remove a moor tile and collect peat tokens. Peat serves double duty as both a resource and an energy source for processing buildings. Getting peat early is smart because you will need energy throughout the game.

walkthrough.title: Player Three's Mistake: Building Too Early
walkthrough.text: Player three sees an appealing building in the display and wants to build it immediately. But they do not have enough resources to pay the full cost. They can only afford the building by spending every resource they have, leaving themselves completely broke. They build it anyway.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Don't Spend Everything at Once
walkthrough.text: Player three now has a nice building but zero resources. They cannot use any processing buildings because they have nothing to process. They will spend the next several turns just gathering basic goods to recover. It would have been better to harvest resources for a round or two first, then build with a cushion to spare.

walkthrough.title: Round Two: Using Buildings
walkthrough.text: A new round begins and the wheel advances again. Player one decides to use a building. They place a Lay Brother on their Farmyard to gain grain. This is the core loop: harvest raw materials from your landscape, then use buildings to convert or leverage them.

walkthrough.title: Player Two: Strategic Patience
walkthrough.text: Player two notices that several goods on the wheel are climbing nicely. Instead of harvesting now, they decide to use a building that does not depend on wheel values. By waiting, those wheel values will be even higher next round. This is smart wheel management.

walkthrough.title: Player Three's Mistake: Ignoring Clergy Management
walkthrough.text: Player three places their second Lay Brother on their new building. That is two of three clergy now deployed. Next turn they place their Prior on a third building. Now all three clergy are out, and Player three must recall all of them before using any building again. They have locked themselves out of the Use a Building action for a full turn.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Manage Your Clergy Carefully
walkthrough.text: With only three clergy members for the entire game, deploying them all at once is a serious mistake. You are stuck unable to use buildings until you spend a turn recalling everyone. Experienced players try to keep at least one clergy member available at all times, especially the Prior, so they always have the option to use a building.

walkthrough.title: Building Your First Processing Chain
walkthrough.text: Let's focus on Player one. They have grain from the Farmyard and wood for energy. They build a Grain Mill and use it to convert grain into flour. This is the start of a processing chain. Next they will want a Bakery to turn flour into bread, which is worth significantly more points.

walkthrough.title: Why Processing Matters
walkthrough.text: Raw goods are worth very little at end of game. Grain by itself scores almost nothing. But grain converted to flour, then flour baked into bread with energy, creates a much more valuable good. The further down a processing chain you go, the more points each good is worth. Always aim to process rather than hoard raw materials.

walkthrough.title: Player Two: Buying a Landscape
walkthrough.text: Player two has been accumulating coins and now buys a coastal landscape strip. They attach it to their heartland, expanding their territory. More territory means more spaces for buildings, and more buildings mean better settlements. Expanding early gives you flexibility for the rest of the game.

walkthrough.title: Player Three's Mistake: Neglecting Territory
walkthrough.text: Player three has been focused entirely on buildings and resources but has not bought any landscape strips. Their heartland is almost full, and they are running out of valid spaces to place new buildings. Meanwhile, Players one and two have room to grow. Expansion should happen early, not as an afterthought.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Expand Your Territory Early
walkthrough.text: Your heartland is small. If you wait too long to buy landscape strips, you will find yourself with no room for the powerful late-game buildings. Buy at least one landscape strip in the first few rounds. The cost in coins is well worth the building space it provides.

walkthrough.title: Approaching Settlement Scoring
walkthrough.text: The round track shows a settlement scoring phase coming up soon. Let's check what each player needs. A settlement requires connected buildings including at least one dwelling. Player one has been building in a cluster, which is good. Player two has a dwelling ready. Player three has buildings scattered with gaps between them.

walkthrough.title: Player Three's Mistake: Scattered Buildings
walkthrough.text: Player three placed buildings wherever there was space without thinking about adjacency. Now their buildings are not connected, and they cannot form a valid settlement. Buildings that are not part of a settlement score reduced or zero points. Player three will miss out on a major scoring opportunity.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Plan Your Building Layout
walkthrough.text: Always think about settlement formation when placing buildings. Connected clusters score far more than scattered buildings. Before you place a building, ask yourself whether it will connect to your existing cluster and contribute to a future settlement. Spatial planning is just as important as resource management in this game.

walkthrough.title: Settlement Scoring in Action
walkthrough.text: The scoring round arrives. Player one forms a settlement with four connected buildings including a dwelling, scoring solid points. Player two forms a smaller settlement but still scores. Player three cannot form a valid settlement at all and scores zero. The gap is already significant.

walkthrough.title: Mid-Game Strategy Shift
walkthrough.text: After the first settlement scoring, the game shifts. New, more powerful buildings become available. Processing chains become more important as you need high-value goods for the final scoring. Player one pivots to building cloisters for adjacency bonuses. Player two focuses on their wine or whiskey chain. Even Player three can recover by clustering buildings from here forward.

walkthrough.title: Using Opponents' Buildings
walkthrough.text: Player two does not have a Bakery but Player one does. Player two places their Prior on Player one's Bakery and pays the usage fee. This lets Player two convert flour to bread without building their own Bakery. It costs coins, but sometimes it is more efficient than building a duplicate. This also blocks Player one from using their own Bakery until Player two's clergy is recalled.

walkthrough.title: Continue Playing
walkthrough.text: You have now seen all four actions in practice, watched the production wheel drive timing decisions, and learned from Player three's common mistakes. Continue playing through the remaining rounds, focusing on building connected clusters, processing goods up the value chain, and preparing for each settlement scoring phase.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: Congratulations! You have walked through the key phases of Ora et Labora. You have seen how the production wheel creates timing tension, how clergy management can make or break your turns, and why spatial planning matters for settlements. Remember: expand early, process your goods, keep a clergy member in reserve, and cluster your buildings.

walkthrough.title: Before You Begin
walkthrough.text:
Before you start a full game, here are three things that can help.

First, check out General Tips for strategy fundamentals and resource management advice.

Second, reference the Appendix for a quick guide to building types, resource chains, and key terms.

And most importantly, ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Have fun!

walkthrough.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
walkthrough.text: Before we get into strategy, here are the mistakes that trip up new players most often. First, spending all your resources on a single building and having nothing left to actually use it. Second, deploying all three clergy at once and locking yourself out of building actions. Third, scattering buildings across your territory instead of clustering them for settlements. Fourth, ignoring the production wheel timing and always harvesting at low values. And fifth, neglecting to buy landscape strips until there is no room left for buildings.

walkthrough.title: Master the Production Wheel
walkthrough.text: The production wheel is the single most important mechanism to understand. Every round, all goods tick up by one. When you take a good, you get the full accumulated value and it resets. The key insight is that you want to time your harvests to get maximum value, but not wait so long that an opponent takes it first. Watch what resources your opponents need and harvest just before they do. If nobody else needs wood, let it climb. If everyone needs clay, grab it before it resets.

walkthrough.title: Clergy Management
walkthrough.text: You have exactly three clergy for the entire game, and managing them is a constant puzzle. The golden rule is to avoid deploying all three at once unless you are sure you will not need to use a building next turn. Keep your Prior in reserve whenever possible, because the Prior is your only way to use opponents' buildings. Think of your clergy as a limited action budget. Each placement should have a clear purpose, not just fill time.

walkthrough.title: Build Processing Chains Early
walkthrough.text: Raw goods score almost nothing at the end of the game. The real points come from processed goods: bread, wine, whiskey, ornaments, books, and relics. Start building your processing chain early so you have time to run materials through it multiple times. A Grain Mill built in round three will process far more flour over the game than one built in round fifteen. Identify which chain you want to pursue and get the buildings in place as soon as you can afford them.

walkthrough.title: Plan for Settlement Scoring
walkthrough.text: Settlement scoring happens at fixed points on the round track. You know exactly when it is coming, so plan for it. Every building you place should be part of a potential settlement cluster. A common mistake is building something powerful in an isolated spot where it cannot connect to other buildings. Before you place any building, look at your grid and ask whether it will be part of a settlement by the next scoring round.

walkthrough.title: Expand Territory Early
walkthrough.text: Your heartland strip is small, and you will quickly run out of valid spaces for buildings. Buy at least one landscape strip in the first few rounds. The coin investment pays for itself many times over because the buildings you place on that land will score points for the rest of the game. Coastal strips are especially valuable because they give you access to water-adjacent terrain for certain buildings.

walkthrough.title: Use Opponents' Buildings Strategically
walkthrough.text: Do not forget that your Prior can use opponents' buildings for a fee. This can save you the cost of building your own copy of an expensive processing building. It also has a defensive benefit: placing your Prior on an opponent's key building blocks them from using it until your clergy is recalled. The fee is usually worth it if the building would cost you far more to construct yourself.

walkthrough.title: Balance Short-Term and Long-Term
walkthrough.text: Ora et Labora is a long game with twenty-four or more rounds, and new players often focus too much on immediate needs. Always keep one eye on the future. What buildings are coming up in the next few rounds? What resources will you need to build them? What does the next settlement scoring require? The best players are always planning two or three rounds ahead while staying flexible enough to react to what opponents do.

summary.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
bullets: Don't spend all resources on one building — keep a cushion
Don't deploy all 3 clergy at once — you'll be locked out
Don't scatter buildings — cluster for settlements
Don't ignore wheel timing — harvest at high values
Don't neglect landscape expansion — buy early

Wait for high values but watch opponents
Harvest just before opponents who need the same good
If nobody else needs it, let it climb

Keep Prior in reserve when possible (only way to use opponents' buildings)

Build processing buildings early for max throughput

Identify your chain early and get buildings in place

Coastal strips especially valuable for water-adjacent buildings

summary.title: Balance Short-Term and Long-Term
bullets: 24+ rounds — don't tunnel on immediate needs
Plan 2-3 rounds ahead for buildings and resources
Watch round track for upcoming buildings and scoring
Stay flexible to react to opponents

walkthrough.title: Production Wheel Mastery
walkthrough.text: Advanced players treat the production wheel as a competitive mini-game. Track what each opponent needs and time your harvests to deny them large payoffs. If an opponent has a Bakery and needs grain, harvest grain before them even if you do not desperately need it. Conversely, if nobody is contesting a resource, let it accumulate to five or six before taking it. The player who consistently harvests at higher values will have a significant resource advantage over the game.

walkthrough.title: Clergy Tempo
walkthrough.text: Think of clergy placement as a tempo game. Ideally, you want to deploy clergy on high-impact buildings and then trigger a recall at a natural break point when you need to harvest or build anyway. Advanced players sometimes intentionally deploy all three clergy in rapid succession to get three powerful building activations, then use the forced recall turn to fell trees or buy a landscape. This turns the recall penalty into a planned action rather than a wasted turn.

walkthrough.title: Settlement Architecture
walkthrough.text: Plan your entire territory layout before you start building in earnest. The best settlements are compact clusters where every building connects and where cloisters sit surrounded by high-value neighbors. Start by placing a dwelling in the center of where you want your settlement, then build outward. When buying landscape strips, think about how they extend your cluster rather than just adding random space. A well-planned territory looks like a tight city block, not a scattered suburb.

walkthrough.title: Processing Chain Optimization
walkthrough.text: The most efficient players build their processing chains in a specific order. First, secure your energy supply by stockpiling peat or wood. Second, build the final stage of your chain, like the Bakery or Winery, because that is where the most value is created. Third, build the intermediate stages. This way, every time you process raw materials, you can immediately push them all the way to the end of the chain in one sequence of building uses, rather than stockpiling intermediate goods that sit around doing nothing.

walkthrough.title: Opponent Building Exploitation
walkthrough.text: Using opponents' buildings is not just a convenience but a strategic weapon. Place your Prior on the building your opponent needs most, right before they plan to use it. This denies them a critical action and forces them to waste a turn. Meanwhile, you get the benefit of a building you did not have to spend resources constructing. In games with experienced players, Prior placement becomes a chess match of blocking and counter-blocking.

walkthrough.title: Cloister Maximization
walkthrough.text: Cloisters are among the highest-scoring buildings in the game because they award bonus points based on adjacent buildings. The key is to place cloisters in the interior of your grid where they can be surrounded on all sides. A cloister with four high-value neighbors can be worth double or triple its base points. Plan your cloister placement several rounds in advance and build the surrounding buildings first so the cloister immediately scores at full value.

walkthrough.title: Late-Game Goods Conversion
walkthrough.text: In the final rounds, shift your focus to accumulating the highest-value goods for end-game conversion. Wine, whiskey, relics, books, and ornaments all convert to victory points at premium rates. A single relic or book can be worth more than many buildings. Plan your final few rounds to run your processing chains one last time, converting everything possible into top-tier goods. Do not waste your last turns on actions that do not directly translate to points.

walkthrough.title: Variant-Specific Strategy
walkthrough.text: France and Ireland play quite differently despite sharing core rules. In France, wine is the premium good, and the cloister set rewards a more religious-building-heavy strategy. Pursue grapes early and build the Winery as a centerpiece. In Ireland, whiskey is king, and the building mix favors a more industrial approach. The grain-to-whiskey chain is shorter than France's wine chain, meaning you can start producing high-value goods sooner but need to produce more volume to keep up. Adjust your opening based on which variant you are playing.

entries.definition: The central mechanism that tracks resource availability. A rotating arm advances each round, increasing every good's production value by one. When a player takes a good, they receive the accumulated amount and the marker resets to zero.
Each player's three workers: one Prior and two Lay Brothers. Placed on buildings to activate them. Once all three are deployed, all must be recalled before any can be placed again.
The most flexible clergy member. Can be placed on any building, including opponents' buildings (with a usage fee). Each player has exactly one Prior.
A basic clergy member that can only be placed on the owning player's buildings. Each player has exactly two Lay Brothers.
When all three of a player's clergy are deployed on buildings, the player must recall all of them before placing any again. This effectively costs a turn of building use.
Each player's starting landscape strip containing initial terrain tiles (forest, moor). The foundation of your territory that you expand throughout the game.

A connected group of buildings in a player's territory that meets specific requirements, including at least one dwelling. Scored at fixed points during the game for victory points based on building values.
Occurs at fixed rounds marked on the round track. Players form settlements from connected buildings and score points. Buildings not in settlements may score zero or reduced points.
Tracks game progress through 24-25 rounds. Shows when new buildings become available and when settlement scoring phases occur.
The coin cost a player must pay to the building's owner when using an opponent's building with the Prior. Varies by building.
An action that removes a forest tile from your landscape to gain wood. The amount gained equals the current production wheel value for wood, which then resets to zero.
An action that removes a moor tile from your landscape to gain peat. The amount gained equals the current production wheel value for peat, which then resets to zero.
An action where you place a clergy member on a building to activate its effect. Own buildings are free; opponents' buildings require the Prior and a usage fee.
An action where you pay a building's resource cost, take it from the display, and place it on a valid terrain space in your landscape grid.
An action where you spend coins to acquire a new landscape strip and attach it to your territory, expanding your building space.
A basic resource harvested by felling forest tiles. Used as a building material and as an energy source for processing buildings.
A basic resource used for building construction. Can be processed into ceramic or bricks at the appropriate building.
A basic resource harvested by cutting moor tiles. Serves as both a building material and an energy source for processing.
A basic resource gained from farming buildings. The start of the grain processing chain: grain to flour to bread. In Ireland, also used for whiskey production.
A basic resource representing farm animals. Can be processed into meat at the slaughterhouse using energy.
Currency used to buy landscape strips, pay building usage fees, and cover some building costs. Not worth victory points directly. Some buildings generate coins when used.
A processed good created from grain at the Grain Mill. An intermediate product that can be further processed into bread at the Bakery.
A high-value processed good created from flour at the Bakery, requiring energy. One of the key goods for end-game points conversion.
A premium processed good available in the France variant. Created from grapes at the Winery. One of the highest-value goods for end-game conversion to victory points.
A premium processed good available in the Ireland variant. Created from grain at the Distillery. One of the highest-value goods for end-game conversion to victory points.
A processed good created from grain. Provides a different conversion path than the flour-bread chain.
A processed good created from livestock at the Slaughterhouse, requiring energy. Worth more VP than raw livestock.
A processed good created from clay at the Pottery, requiring energy. One of two possible outputs from clay processing.
A processed good created from clay. Used as a building material for some advanced buildings and also worth VP.
A processed good derived from grain processing. Used in some building costs and has modest VP value.
A high-value processed good. One of the premium goods that converts to victory points at a favorable rate during end-game scoring.
A high-value processed good often associated with religious buildings. Converts to victory points at a premium rate during end-game scoring.
The highest-value processed good in the game. Extremely valuable for end-game VP conversion. Difficult to produce but worth the investment.
A requirement for many processing buildings. Wood, peat, and coal can all be spent as energy sources. You must have energy available to run processing chains.
A landscape tile that can be removed via the Fell Trees action to gain wood. Part of your starting heartland.
A landscape tile that can be removed via the Cut Peat action to gain peat. Part of your starting heartland.
A purchasable landscape strip providing water-adjacent terrain. Some buildings require coastal placement. Expands your territory for more building room.
A purchasable landscape strip providing elevated terrain. Certain buildings require hillside placement. Adds building space to your territory.
A purchasable landscape strip providing flat terrain. Versatile for many building types. Adds building space to your territory.
A building category that generates basic goods when activated by clergy. Examples include the Farmyard for grain and various resource-generating structures.
A building category that converts raw goods into more valuable processed goods, often requiring energy. Examples include the Grain Mill, Bakery, Slaughterhouse, and Pottery.

A religious building that provides powerful abilities and scores bonus victory points based on the buildings adjacent to it. Place in grid interior for maximum adjacency bonuses. France and Ireland have different cloister sets.
A building type required for settlement formation. Every valid settlement must include at least one dwelling. Building dwellings early ensures you can score at settlement phases.
A processing building that converts grain into flour. Part of the grain processing chain (grain to flour to bread).
A processing building that converts flour into bread using energy. Bread is one of the higher-value goods for scoring.
A processing building that converts livestock into meat using energy. Meat is worth more VP than raw livestock.
A processing building that converts clay into ceramic or bricks using energy.
A processing building available in the France variant that converts grapes into wine. Wine is one of the most valuable goods for end-game scoring.
A processing building available in the Ireland variant that converts grain into whiskey. Whiskey is one of the most valuable goods for end-game scoring.
A basic production building that generates grain or livestock when activated. One of the starting buildings available to all players.
One of two game variants featuring wine production, a specific set of cloisters, and emphasis on ornamental goods. Uses a distinct building set but identical core rules.

One of two game variants featuring whiskey production and a different building mix. The grain-to-whiskey chain is shorter than France's wine chain. Uses a distinct building set but identical core rules.
A tile placed on a group of connected buildings during settlement scoring to mark them as a scored settlement. Each settlement tile has specific building requirements.
The printed victory point value on each building tile. Scored at end of game as one of the three main scoring categories.
At end of game, high-value goods (wine, whiskey, relics, books, ornaments) can be converted to VP at specified rates. One of the three main scoring categories.
